description: One of Nigeria's greatest soups. Ground melon seeds fried or cooked in a rich palm oil and pepper base with assorted meat and fish. Dense, nutty, deeply savoury. The kind of soup that makes swallow disappear.

ingredients.name: ground egusi (melon seeds)
ingredients.note: Buy already ground or grind whole seeds yourself. The finer the grind, the smoother the egusi lumps. Coarse grind gives chunkier texture — both are valid.

ingredients.name: palm oil
ingredients.note: Do not substitute. The combination of egusi and palm oil is fundamental to this soup. Use good quality — reddish-orange, not dark brown.

ingredients.name: beef or assorted meat
ingredients.note: Parboil with seasoning first, fry to colour, set aside. Ponmo, tripe, and beef chunks all work well together here.

ingredients.name: stockfish (okporoko)
ingredients.note: Soak in boiling water for 30 minutes to soften. Debone. Essential for authentic depth — hard to replicate.

ingredients.name: smoked fish
ingredients.note: Debone and flake. Adds a smoky, savoury layer.

ingredients.name: fresh or frozen spinach
ingredients.note: Or bitter leaf (ewuro) — washed thoroughly and squeezed to reduce bitterness. Or pumpkin leaf (ugwu). Add at the very end.

ingredients.name: plum tomatoes
ingredients.note: Part of the pepper base. Not as dominant as in stew — this is background flavour.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Egusi carries heat well. 2 is a good starting point.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 blended into the pepper base, 1 finely chopped to mix with the egusi.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Crayfish is as important as egusi itself in this soup. Do not reduce the quantity.

ingredients.name: locust beans (iru/dawadawa)
ingredients.note: Rinse briefly. Adds fermented depth that takes the soup from good to extraordinary.

ingredients.name: meat stock
ingredients.note: From parboiling the meat. The richer the stock, the better the soup.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: Crush before adding. Season in stages.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season at the end — smoked fish and stockfish contribute salt.

steps.title: Prepare the Meats and Stock
steps.instruction: Season beef and other meats with curry, thyme, seasoning cube, onion, and salt. Parboil until 75% cooked. Reserve ALL the stock. Fry the parboiled meat in hot oil until golden-brown. Set aside. If using stockfish, soak in boiling water for 30 minutes — debone and break into large pieces. Debone and flake the smoked fish. Have all proteins ready before starting the soup.
steps.sensory_cue: Fried meat should be golden-brown and smell deeply savoury. Stock should be dark and rich. Stockfish after soaking should be soft enough to debone without fighting it.
steps.failure_risk: Weak, watery stock makes a weak egusi soup. Use minimal water when parboiling — barely covering the meat concentrates the stock significantly.

steps.title: Blend the Pepper Base
steps.instruction: Blend tomatoes, tatashe, scotch bonnet, and 1 onion together until smooth. This pepper base should be fairly smooth — egusi soup does not need a chunky base. Set aside. Finely chop or grate the second onion and mix it directly into the ground egusi with a small amount of water — just enough to form a thick, rough paste. This is the egusi mix.
steps.sensory_cue: The pepper blend should be smooth and vibrant red-orange. The egusi paste with onion should smell nutty and slightly raw — like ground seeds.
steps.failure_risk: Adding too much water to the egusi paste makes it difficult to fry properly — it will splatter dangerously in the hot palm oil. The paste should be thick and hold its shape.

steps.title: Heat the Palm Oil and Fry Aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a large heavy pot over medium heat until it melts and clears — do not let it smoke or darken. Add locust beans if using and fry for 1 minute. Add ground crayfish and stir for 30 seconds. Pour in the blended pepper base. Fry over medium-high heat, stirring frequently, until the pepper base reduces, darkens, and the oil begins to float to the surface — about 10–15 minutes.
steps.sensory_cue: Palm oil should be clear orange, not dark. After frying, the pepper base should look reduced, darker, and the palm oil visible on the surface. The crayfish and locust beans create a deeply savoury, funky aroma.
steps.failure_risk: Burnt palm oil is the most common egusi soup mistake. Medium heat only at this stage. Dark, smoking palm oil tastes harsh and ruins the soup.

steps.title: Fry the Egusi
steps.instruction: Add the egusi paste into the fried pepper base in the pot. Stir to combine. Now — do not stir for 3 minutes. Let the egusi sit and fry against the hot oil and pepper base, forming a slightly crusted layer at the bottom. After 3 minutes, stir and break it up. Let it sit again for 2 minutes. Repeat this fry-and-break cycle 3 times total. This frying is what develops the nutty, deep egusi flavour.
steps.sensory_cue: The egusi should go from pale yellow-white to a deeper golden colour. It should smell nutty and toasty — like roasting seeds. Small golden-brown lumps forming is exactly right.
steps.failure_risk: Stirring constantly prevents the egusi from frying and developing flavour — you get pale, bland, gummy egusi. The controlled fry-and-break method is what separates good egusi soup from great egusi soup.

steps.title: Add Stock and Cook the Egusi
steps.instruction: Pour in the meat stock gradually — add 1 cup at a time, stirring after each addition. The egusi will absorb the liquid and swell. Add enough stock to create a thick, rich soup consistency — not watery, not stiff. Add the fried meat, stockfish, and smoked fish. Stir gently. Add crushed seasoning cubes. Bring to a simmer over medium heat.
steps.sensory_cue: The soup should look thick and deeply coloured — orange-red from the palm oil and pepper, with golden egusi lumps distributed throughout. The meat and fish should be half-submerged. The aroma should be full, rich, and complex.
steps.failure_risk: Adding all the stock at once before the egusi is properly fried results in pale, gummy, undercooked egusi with no depth of flavour. Add stock gradually and let the egusi absorb it.

steps.title: Simmer, Season and Add Leaves
steps.instruction: Cover and simmer on low heat for 20 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes. The egusi will fully absorb the stock and the soup will thicken. Taste and adjust salt now. If using spinach, bitter leaf, or pumpkin leaf — add it now and stir in. Cook uncovered for a final 5 minutes. The leaf should be bright and just wilted — not overcooked and grey.
steps.sensory_cue: Finished egusi soup should be thick, glossy, and deeply orange-red. The egusi lumps should be fully cooked — golden, nutty, and firm but not hard. Leaves should be bright green and just wilted. One taste should deliver sweetness from the palm oil, heat from the pepper, nuttiness from the egusi, and umami from the crayfish and fish all at once.
steps.failure_risk: Overcooking the leaves turns them grey, slimy, and bitter. Add them at the very end — 5 minutes maximum. If using bitter leaf, make sure it has been thoroughly washed and squeezed to remove excess bitterness before adding.
